1.exposition: The seventh bowl has fallen, and now one of the seven bowl-angels draws John aside for a closer look at the doom already announced: Come, I will show you the judgment of the great prostitute who is seated on many waters. What he sees is deliberately obscene — a woman gorgeously arrayed in purple and scarlet, glittering with gold and jewels, holding a golden cup full of abominations, and yet drunk with the blood of the saints. On her forehead is a name, Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes. The vision unmasks seductive worldly power: what dazzles the nations with wealth and beauty is, at its heart, idolatry and murder. The seer who once wept that none was worthy to open the scroll now stares in great amazement — dazzled for a moment, so that the angel must correct even him.
1.original_language: The woman is a pornē (“prostitute, harlot”), and her sin is porneia — the prophets’ standing metaphor for covenant unfaithfulness and idolatry, not merely private vice. Her name is prefaced by mystērion (“mystery”): the word signals that “Babylon” is to be read symbolically and spiritually — a hidden meaning to be discerned, not the literal city on the Euphrates. Her scarlet (kokkinon) and purple (porphyroun) are the dye-colours of empire and luxury, while the golden potērion (“cup”), fair without and foul within, inverts Jeremiah’s golden cup in the Lord’s hand that made all the earth drunk.
1.historical_context: John’s harlot is stitched together from the prophets. Nineveh and Tyre were pictured as prostitutes who seduce the nations by commerce and cult (Nahum 3:4; Isaiah 23:17), and above all Babylon was the “golden city” who “dwells by many waters” (Jeremiah 51:13) — an image the chapter itself decodes as peoples and nations. For John’s first readers the referent was unmistakable: imperial Rome, purple-clad and gold-laden and drunk on the blood of the martyrs. The Reformation expositors (reformed Gill, puritan Henry, wesleyan-arminian Wesley) extended the figure to apostate, persecuting religion — papal Rome as the antitype of ancient Babylon — while preterist readers hold to pagan Rome. Beneath both lies the enduring type: any civilization that gilds its idolatry and sheds the blood of the faithful.
1.christ: The harlot is the counterfeit bride. Over against her stands the wife of the Lamb, the new Jerusalem, shown to John by one of these same bowl-angels — but from a great mountain, not a wilderness (Revelation 21:9-10). Babylon’s cup of abomination is the dark twin of the cup of the new covenant in Christ’s blood (Luke 22:20). And her drunkenness on the blood of the martyrs of Jesus is finally enmity against Christ himself in his members — the persecutor on the Damascus road heard, “why are you persecuting me?” (Acts 9:4).

verses.note: One of the seven bowl-angels turns aside to show John the judgment of the great prostitute who sits on many waters. grammatical-historical Robertson notes that krima (“judgment”) is the very word used for Babylon’s doom in Jeremiah 51:9: the sentence pronounced in 16:19 and already announced in 14:8 is now unfolded. The “many waters” are borrowed from Babylon enthroned on the Euphrates and its canals (Jeremiah 51:13); grammatical-historical Barnes hears the prophets’ city-as-woman convention — a faithful city a virgin, a corrupt one a harlot (Isaiah 1:21).

Her corruption is twofold: the kings of the earth committed fornication with her, and the earth-dwellers were made drunk with the wine of her fornication. grammatical-historical Barnes takes it as spiritual adultery — Rome seducing the rulers into her idolatry and corruption; Robertson, following Swete, hears the price of her favour, the vassal kings purchasing her patronage and with it her vices. wesleyan-arminian Wesley presses the figure hard: no wine intoxicates its drinkers more thoroughly than the false zeal the great harlot pours out. The seduction reaches rulers and common people alike (14:8).

In the Spirit John is carried into a wilderness, and sees the woman seated on a scarlet beast covered with blasphemous names, with seven heads and ten horns — the same beast as Revelation 13. grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the irony: for all her dazzling surroundings she is still in a desert, and she rides the world-power rather than reigning in her own right — drawing her whole support from the beast that carries her. wesleyan-arminian Wesley distinguishes the two: the woman is the city of Rome with its pomp, the beast the persecuting power beneath her. The scarlet bespeaks at once imperial splendour and blood-guilt.

The array is catalogued piece by piece — purple and scarlet, gold and jewels and pearls, and a golden cup in her hand full of abominations. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the cup seems to hold wine but is in truth full of abominable drugs — allurement without, corruption within. grammatical-historical Ellicott hears behind it Jeremiah’s golden cup that made all the earth drunk. Earliest of all, the latin-fathers Bede read the golden cup as hypocrisy itself: like those who outwardly appear righteous to men but within are full of all uncleanness, she is fair on the surface and foul beneath.

On her forehead, as brazen harlots wore their names, is written her identity: MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF PROSTITUTES AND OF EARTH’S ABOMINATIONS. reformed Gill gathers the classical witnesses that prostitutes labelled their brows — an image of open, shameless idolatry. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson insist the prefaced word mystery makes the name symbolic: this is not the literal Babylon but a city like her, to be read “mystically” as Rome. Here the great crux of the chapter surfaces — the reformed Gill, puritan Henry, and wesleyan-arminian Wesley read the harlot as papal Rome, the apostate church; preterist interpreters as pagan imperial Rome; others as the apostate world-system of every age.

The woman is drunk with the blood of the saints and the blood of the martyrs of Jesus. reformed Gill finds the sight monstrous — not wine but blood, and not her own but that of God’s holy ones. puritan Henry sharpens it: she drank their blood so greedily that she was satiated yet never satisfied. And John, who should have recoiled, instead marvels with great amazement — briefly dazzled by her splendour, so that the angel must ask why he wonders and set him straight (v.7).

witnesses.point: The harlot is a city portrayed as an abandoned woman; her gilded attire and golden cup allure the nations, while the cup itself is full of abomination — splendour without, corruption within.
The prefaced word “mystery” makes “Babylon” a spiritual name for Rome; the harlot-city sits astride the beast, the Roman world-power — as Nahum called Nineveh and Isaiah called Tyre a harlot.
Harlots wrote their names on their foreheads — an image of the brazen, public idolatry of the woman, monstrously drunk not with wine but with the blood of the saints.
The golden cup full of abominations is hypocrisy: outwardly appearing righteous before men, inwardly full of all uncleanness.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The harlot fuses the picture of a prostitute with that of a city — Rome enthroned as the goddess Roma — seducing kings and merchants through the wealth and luxury of her trade; her glittering commerce is the wine that intoxicates the nations, and her splendour is built on violence against the saints.

1.application: Babylon’s danger to the church is not only her sword but her cup. The seer himself is briefly dazzled; how much more are we, surrounded by a civilization that gilds its idolatries in beauty and wealth. Revelation trains the eye to see glory and gore together — and later makes the practical demand explicit: Come out of her, my people.

7.exposition: John’s amazement becomes the hinge: I will tell you the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carries her. What follows is an angelic decoding, the form familiar from Daniel 7 and Zechariah 4, where the seer asks and a heavenly figure reads the symbols. The beast was, and is not, and is about to rise from the abyss and go to destruction; its seven heads are seven mountains and also seven kings; its ten horns are ten kings who reign one hour with the beast. Their combined assault on the Lamb is named, and its outcome given in advance: the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings. Then the sovereign irony — the beast and its kings turn on the prostitute and burn her, unwittingly carrying out God’s purpose. Evil divides and devours itself, and the great city is unmasked as the power that reigns over the kings of the earth.
7.original_language: The beast’s self-description — “was, and is not, and is about to come” — is a deliberate parody of the divine name that opens the book: God “who is and who was and who is to come” (Revelation 1:8; 1:4). The beast counterfeits eternity, aping death and resurrection, yet its future is not “to come” but eis apōleian — “into destruction.” The seven heads carry a double sense: hepta orē (“seven mountains,” the seven hills) and hepta basileis (“seven kings”). The angel prefaces the riddle with “here is the mind that has wisdom”, flagging that the number resists a flat reading. At the climax the Lamb bears the title kyrios kyriōn kai basileus basileōn — “Lord of lords and King of kings” (v.14).
7.historical_context: Seven mountains is an almost transparent pointer to Rome, the city famed across the ancient world for its seven hills; no first-century reader could miss it. The beast that revives likely also plays on the Nero redivivus legend — the dread that the dead persecutor would return at the head of an army. The seven kings, of whom “five have fallen, one is, the other has not yet come,” are the chapter’s most debated riddle, and the witnesses divide honestly. grammatical-historical Barnes reads them as successive forms or dynasties of Roman government; preterist readers count individual emperors — but the schemes differ over where to begin (Julius or Augustus) and whether to include the brief usurpers Galba, Otho, and Vitellius of AD 69, so no single tally has ever commanded the field. A third stream takes the number symbolically: seven is completeness, so the heads signify the whole succession of oppressive world-power, of which Rome is the present embodiment. The ten horns recall the ten-horned fourth beast of Daniel 7:7, 24.
7.christ: The heart of the explanation is a single doxological line: the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings. The title belongs in the Old Testament to the LORD himself (Deuteronomy 10:17; Psalms 136:3), and it is borne here by the slain Lamb — repeated at his triumphant appearing (Revelation 19:16). The beast that “was, and is not” is the dark parody of the one who was dead and is alive forevermore; the counterfeit goes to destruction, the true King reigns. And those who share his victory are named called and chosen and faithful — election, calling, and persevering faith, all secured in him.

verses.note: Seeing John’s astonishment, the angel offers the key: Why do you marvel? I will tell you the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carries her. puritan Henry notes that one of the bowl-angels now unfolds the meaning of the earlier vision of the beast; grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson add that both the harlot and the beast beneath her, with its seven heads and ten horns, are now to be explained (vv.8-18).

The beast was, and is not, and is about to rise from the abyss and go to destruction, and the earth-dwellers whose names are not in the book of life will marvel at it. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the riddle as the fall and revival of the persecuting power (cf. 13:3): preterists hear the Nero redivivus expectation, historicists the eclipse and return of the antichristian power, but all agree its end is perdition. reformed Gill lingers on the paradox — it was, is not, and yet shall be — the mysterious survival of a doomed power, which only the lost, never the elect, are deceived to adore (13:8).

Here is the mind that has wisdom: the seven heads are seven mountains on which the woman sits. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott record the near-universal verdict: the seven hills identify the city beyond mistaking as Rome. The call for “wisdom” (as at 13:18) both invites the reader to discern and warns that the symbol carries more than one layer, for the heads are at once mountains and, in the next breath, kings.

There are seven kings: five have fallen, one is, the other has not yet come; and when he comes he must remain a little while. This is the chapter’s famous crux, and the witnesses hold it open. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the seven as successive forms or dynasties of Roman government — five passed, the sixth then reigning, a seventh yet to come and short-lived. Preterist readers count individual emperors from Augustus (or Julius), placing the writing under the sixth; but because the schemes differ over the starting point and over the three brief usurpers of AD 69, no single identification has ever settled the matter. A third reading takes the number symbolically — seven for completeness — so that the heads picture the whole succession of world-powers rather than a cipher to be cracked. The sequence at least sets the vision at the sixth head, with a brief seventh still ahead.

The beast that was and is not is itself an eighth, and yet belongs to the seven, and goes to destruction. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the paradox: the revived beast is reckoned an eighth power, yet springs from the seven and is of their kind — a returning emperor to preterists (Nero come again), the final form of the antichristian power to historicists. However it is counted, the repeated refrain fixes its doom: it goes into perdition (v.8).

The ten horns are ten kings who have not yet received a kingdom, but receive authority as kings for one hour with the beast. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note they were still future when John wrote; historicists identify them with the kingdoms that rose from the divided Roman empire. Their power is brief — “one hour” — and wholly at the beast’s service, recalling the ten horns of the fourth beast in Daniel 7:7.

These are of one mind, and hand over their power and authority to the beast. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott mark their unnerving concord: a confederacy of one will, pooling their strength in the antichristian power for the war that follows (v.14). Their unity, unlike the church’s, serves not worship but rebellion.

They will make war on the Lamb, and the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings. grammatical-historical Barnes and puritan Henry ground the certainty of victory in the title itself — the same borne at his appearing in 19:16: no king or beast can prevail against the King of kings. And those with him are called, and chosen, and faithful — Henry marks the threefold seal of the true saints, who share the Lamb’s triumph. The battle’s outcome is declared long before the sword of Revelation 19 is drawn.

The angel decodes the last symbol: the waters where the prostitute sits are peoples and multitudes and nations and tongues. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the fourfold phrase for the whole mass of mankind — her seat on the waters (v.1) marks the vast reach of her rule; puritan Henry, that her seduction extends over the populous world. Her dominion is wide — and about to be turned against her.

The turn comes with brutal suddenness: the ten horns and the beast will hate the prostitute, make her desolate and naked, devour her flesh, and burn her with fire. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the reversal — the very powers that bore her become the instruments of her ruin. reformed Jamieson, Fausset and Brown hear the law’s penalty for the harlot in the burning (cf. Leviticus 21:9), consummated in the burning of Babylon (18:8). It is the prophets’ theme of evil consuming itself, as Oholibah’s lovers turned on her (Ezekiel 23:22-25).

Behind the treachery stands a deeper hand: God has put it into their hearts to carry out his purpose, to be of one mind and hand over their kingdom to the beast, until the words of God are fulfilled. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott find here one of Scripture’s plainest statements of God’s sovereignty over his enemies — without excusing their malice, he bends even their agreement and their cruelty to accomplish his word, as with Joseph’s brothers (Genesis 50:20) and the rulers gathered against Christ (Acts 4:27-28). Their alliance runs only “until” his words are done.

The explanation ends where the vision began: the woman you saw is the great city that reigns over the kings of the earth. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill identify her with Babylon (16:19; v.5) — and in John’s day one city alone reigned over the kings of the earth: Rome. Historicists press the identification to papal Rome, preterists to the imperial city; either way the harlot is a ruling power, now doomed, whose fall the next chapter both laments and celebrates.

witnesses.point: Reads the seven heads as successive forms or dynasties of Roman government, placing the writing at the sixth, and the ten horns as the kingdoms of the divided empire — yet insists the seven hills mark the city as Rome.
The beast’s “was, and is not, and is to come” plays on the expectation of its revival; the seven mountains point unmistakably to Rome.
The beast is both an eighth and of the seven — the revived persecuting power; the ten kings a short-lived confederacy of one mind that surrenders its strength to it.
The Lamb overcomes because he is Lord of lords and King of kings; those with him are the called, chosen, and faithful who share his triumph.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: While “seven mountains” points plainly to Rome, the seven kings resist a tidy solution — depending on which emperor one starts from and whether the brief claimants of AD 69 are counted, the schemes diverge — so the number likely works as much schematically, signifying a complete span of rulers, as it does as a precise chronological cipher.

7.application: The powers arrayed against the church are not a stable, eternal order but a self-destructing coalition under God’s hand — a beast that turns on its own harlot, kings who serve a purpose they never intended. The comfort is immense and the summons plain: the Lamb’s victory is already declared, so his people are called not to panic but to be with him — called and chosen and faithful.

_meta.title: The Great Prostitute and the Scarlet Beast
reflection: The vision unmasks a power gorgeous on the surface and bloody underneath (vv.4–6). Where are you tempted to be dazzled by a wealth or beauty that Scripture would have you see as Babylon?

Even John is briefly amazed by the harlot, and the angel must correct him (v.6). What would it take to see your own culture’s cup — its comforts and its costs — clearly enough to heed the later call, “Come out of her, my people” (Revelation 18:4)?

The Lamb’s victory is announced before the battle is joined, and God bends even his enemies’ malice to his purpose (v.14, 17). How does knowing the outcome change the way you endure pressure now, as one of the “called and chosen and faithful”?
